item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can agree with a lot of the earlier criticism of this, but this also elicited an emotional reaction from me. Also I know people who are… pretty uncomfortably similar to this entity.

Gave me the meta-squicks. +1.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I'm good with this. Thanks!

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: author approved of edits, timer removed

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This SCP mentions Kiryu Labs, which has a hub. I propose linking these two articles together.

Editing this:

SCP-2239 is to remain under Kiryu Labs supervision to encourage it to maintain its current form.

To this:

SCP-2239 is to remain under Kiryu Labs supervision to encourage it to maintain its current form.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love him what a good boy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a cute little story. I really did like the interview. The fish(?) was cute too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: >implying 682 is not already a sympathy-682

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Totally a sympathy-682.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I can get that.

Besides, boiled down, everything this thing communicates/writes/says is essentially "please take care of me ;^;"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing being unable to control its ability(ies) is a focal point, yeah.

Spoilers or ramblings?
bit of both, I guess.

The thing is only a butterfly bamboo shark that can write kanji at the moment because that's a form ideally matched to its current caretaker (Researcher Kiryu is half Chinese, half Japanese, likes butterflies and sharks, and studies biological anomalies).

The Foundation's seen how many forms this thing is capable of, and now that it can communicate, it's worth keeping the shark in that form instead of testing with various personnel until they get another form that can communicate.

My headcanon is that the thing doesn't actually remember anything. But it doesn't know that. Its memories are unstable, and are warped as necessary, just as its body is, to trigger sympathy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, that's what I thought. FWIW, I would consider that "talking" more or less, but YMMV.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It writes kanji/characters in the sand in response to spoken queries. At least, it does when it's in its current form.

Not quite spoilers, but eh
hide

Its appearance isn't the only thing that automatically alters to try and get those around it to invest time and care in it. Any attempts to microchip/tag/put some sort of ownership to it fails between form swaps, and it is always happy to move on to someone else if necessary. It never really belonged to anyone, and it's never been able to control how its appearance, body, and intelligence change in response to those around it. The only constant it is allowed is the amae emotion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It communicates, yes? Or am I missing some subtle clue that this information is unreliable?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Inclined to agree, and I'll also add the expository interview to the list of things-by-rote this does.

I look for more creativity in articles these days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the uncontrollability of the anomaly sells this. It's a good feels article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Likely moot point, but the thing doesn't talk. If the concept doesn't interest you though, then there's no point in me unloading all the subtle things I tried to add with this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Djoric pretty much nailed it on the head. Besides adhering to all your standard writing hallmarks, the attempt at making an emotional connection (to a talking bamboo shark, nonetheless) is weak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't write Zyn Kiryu nearly as often as Mark, so I can't say I'm surprised.

I'm sorry you didn't enjoy the article. Short of overhauling it to your taste though, I'm not sure what can be done. I'll take the downvote, and hope to do better next time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are two?

That is to say, I know that there are two, but they feel completely interchangeable to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(I generally find Kiryu to be a rather boring character, personally, take that for what you will),

Both of them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted.

This feels…rote. Like it was picking things off of a checklist. Talking animal, check. Feels bordering on melodrama, check. Offhand Kiryu mention for extra Zyn points. And while none of these by themselves are bad (I generally find Kiryu to be a rather boring character, personally, take that for what you will), when put together here they feel thoroughly uninspired. Like someone was going out of their way to write the most Zyn-like article they could.

A rote performance competently written. Enough to stick around, but not enough to be remembered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I was rolling my eyes, "Great. Another mighty, morphing, power creature.", but this had depth of story that pulled me in and a different take on the morphing mechanism. It wasn't overly complex, long-winded, or confusing; Just a well written SCP. (Plus, I got a bit of a soft-spot for scips who don't mind being locked-up in the bowels of the Foundation complex).

+1

BTW - What a great list of people that helped refine this scip in one way or another. No wonder this was so well written (textbook).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just realized something.

This is 682, if 682 just wanted to be loved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's just something about reading this sharks dialogue in a deep, brooding voice that makes me chuckle. I don't know. Like, it's not badly written, but taken out of context it could be interpreted in that sorta angsty way. Nevertheless I like this thing a lot. Goodjob.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Troubled buttershark is cute.

item.title: Author Notes
item.content:encoded:
Many thanks to these awesome people (if I forgot anyone, please remind me!):

  • EldrichCyanide, Dr_Leonard, Dexanote and for looking over it in chat
  • Riemann and Dmatix for being tough on the plot development, especially interview
  • Eskobar and Photosynthetic for wording
  • FiestaSheep and Davsto who gave some animal ideas
  • Wogglebug for overview and phrasing
  • MisterFlames for help with feel and cat breed suggestion
  • Vivax for help with SCIENCE and teaching me bauplan and for the patience to properly convey the feels.
  • Drewbear for helping with emotion evocation
  • Cimmerian for edits and trimming (oh man lots of trimming)
  • MacLeod and Eta Carinae for feedback in the forums!
  • Silber for the name. Silber is good at naming.
Author ramblings
Marp

Also known as “man, Zyn misses that one neighborhood cat that used to visit her and her friend in the evenings”. Plants are nice and about all I have time for, but cuddling and doting on a plant feels silly and a little sad.

This sort of grew out of a lot of things. One was the thought, “no one will look twice if you see a cat you don’t know walking around and pet it or cuddle it, but no one does that to a person they don’t know”. And yet people find Tesla strange for loving a pigeon.

There was also an article about aquarium sharks I read awhile back that I’d wanted to write something alluding to at one point.

And also there was that video of a swallow crying when its mate died. Animal emotion is something I wonder a lot about. I wonder how those experiences compare to those of humans. Is simplicity preferable? How does depth factor in?

Edit because can't emphasize enough: the interview went through a ton of revisions, with a bunch of reviewers. Mood was changed, it went from blunt to emo teenager to too obfuscated etc. etc. Many thanks for the patience and the advice, you all.

Spoiler alert
Alert alert.

It’s a vicious cycle. The less “happy” this thing is, the more animal-like it regresses into acting, because that’s the only way it knows how to get more of that unconditional human affection it has always craved. Being an animal for so long takes its toll.

This poor thing has no control over any of its abilities. It just knows what it wants… or at least it thinks it does.

The picture is from the Brown-banded bamboo shark Wikipedia article, and licensed under Wikimedia Commons.
